title: How to Turn off Green Dot on iPhone 17 / 17 Pro
transcript: Hi everyone. In this short video, I will talk about the green dot on your iPhone 17 and iPhone 17 Pro. The green dot shows up in here whenever an app is using your camera. This is a privacy feature in iOS that lets you know when apps are using your camera. Currently, Apple does not provide a way to disable this indicator. So, it will always appear when the camera is active. Now, I scroll down to open the control center. [music] And as you can see, my camera app is uses the camera. If I close the app, [music] it will disappear. Now go to the settings, then privacy and security and camera. Here you can see which apps have access to your camera. If you turn off access for an app, then the green dot will no longer appear for that app. But keep in mind, you won't be able to use the camera with that app anymore. As last resort, try restarting your phone. That's it. We are done. At the end of my video, let me ask you a small favor. As you can see, most of my viewers are not [music] subscribed. title: iPhone 17 Pro Max ou Sony ZV-E10 II: Qual escolher?
transcript: Between buying the iPhone 17 Pro Max or an entry-level camera like the Sony ZV10, which would you choose?  This decision can be quite complicated when you only have the budget for one of the two.  So, in this video I want to show you whether it makes more sense for you to buy the iPhone 17 Pro Max, which will have many more uses than just photography and video, or the Sony CVE1 Mark 2, which is also excellent at what it does, but is dedicated only to photos and videos.  So, which one will you choose? [music] Hey, what's up?  My name is André, and here I like to talk about technology and audiovisual media.  If you enjoy this type of content, then consider subscribing to the channel.  And I had the idea of ​​bringing a kind of comparison to this video, where I would really try to take very similar photos and videos with the Sony ZVE10 Mark 2 and the iPhone 17 Pro Max. However, this task was not easy at all and my time was very limited, so that wasn't possible.  But anyway, what I've done here is to provide an overview of points you should consider in order to decide whether to buy an iPhone or an entry-level camera like the ZV10 to meet your goals and needs, because in fact we have a set of pros and cons on both sides, and we need to be clear about what really makes sense for our needs.  So that's what we're going to talk about in today's video.  [music] And to make it easier, I defined eight criteria that we should consider before choosing whether we're going to use one or the other.  And for each of these criteria, I assigned a score from one to three.  This way, it will be very easy for you to understand what is acceptable, good, or excellent for each criterion.  And the first criterion to consider is portability and setup, and in this respect the iPhone scores three points, because it's a device you can put in your pocket, carry anywhere easily, and you don't have to worry about setup, because all the lenses are always available and you don't have to worry about batteries or anything like that, just go out with your phone charged and it's ready to use.  When we talk about the Sony EV10, even though it's a compact camera, you'll always need to carry it in a bag or backpack, along with batteries, because a single battery may not be enough.  And also the lenses, if you only have the kit lens, then you'll have a battery, the kit lens, the camera to charge along with the charger, just like with the iPhone. But obviously it's not something you can easily carry in your pocket, so you'll always need a dedicated camera bag or backpack. Therefore, for this reason, in this first criterion, the iPhone earns three points, while the Sony ZV10 earns only one point.  And the next point is about quality and latitude, and when we talk about quality, we are concerned with sharpness, color reproduction, and also the amount of noise in the image, depending on the amount of light in the environment, whether it is able to capture good images, even in more precarious lighting conditions.  As for latitude, it's more about the camera's ability to capture images in slightly more challenging scenarios, such as bright lights, low lights, for example, when filming against a window.  Here I chose this setting which has more balanced lighting, but for example, if I come to this side, look, if I capture this scene with the window in the background, you can see that here in the background I practically don't have any details of what's happening outside, because the interior light is lower than the sunlight.  And competing with this amount of light here is really difficult.  I would need a much brighter light, which is not the case for me.  I have a 60W light here. So, going back to the normal scenario, you can now see that I needed to point the camera at an area where I could get more balanced lighting so that no part would be too overexposed.  I even have a video I made recently where I talk about the iPhone 17 Pro Max, and I recorded it with that phone, and you can see that I never filmed it facing that port, and it still had that problem of the Dynamic Range not being sufficient.  But that's pretty much the same with the EVE10 as well.  But the question is this: it's about our ability to capture this information in both high and low light conditions.  And both are more or less similar in this scenario, but also considering that this is a more professional use, where in both devices you will record in the log profile to be able to edit and retrieve this information during editing.  In other words, after recording, which is the post-production phase, you will process these images and be able to recover this information.  Now, if you record in automatic mode on both devices, then the behavior will be different.  So, despite both cameras putting on a strange show, I still believe the Sony V10 will win this competition in this area because it has an APS-C sensor that is much larger than the iPhone's sensor.  So, it's much easier to deal with some variations in light or low light, and even the issue of depth of field, which is the ability to make the background more blurred, more creamy.  This is not something that depends solely on the lens, but also on the sensor size.  So, in that case, I would give the iPhone a score of two and the ZVE1 Mark 2 a three, because the sensor size really matters a lot in this case, and I think that's where the camera comes out on top.  And in this third topic, we'll talk about lenses and looks. Starting with the iPhone, it comes with a set of three lenses: the widest, which is the ultrawide, is 13mm, while the wide lens is 24mm and the telephoto lens is 100mm.  Apple also says that you supposedly have more lenses because, since the sensor has 48 megapixels, if you crop it, you still have very good quality, which would be compatible, for example, with a 200mm telephoto lens and with a 28mm and 35mm wide-angle lens.  This cropping technique does simulate 28mm, 35mm, or even 200mm, but it's not entirely accurate, since we're talking about a digital crop of the image.  With the Sony CV10, you could have an endless variety of lenses, but that abundance would be expensive. Unfortunately, buying contact lenses isn't cheap.  So, since it's probably impossible to find a lens that has that range from 13mm to 200mm, you need more than one lens to actually be compatible with what the iPhone offers. In terms of aesthetics, I think both cameras deliver really good image quality.  I really like the quality I see in both. And what will actually change is the optical quality, which, for example, you can see in some of these images I 'm showing you, that this blurred background is easier to achieve on a camera with a larger sensor.  So, it manages to deliver even more quality with this blurred background, which, of course, isn't all about blurred backgrounds, right? We have other characteristics that we need to consider for the final image quality.  There are times when this excessive blur is even a hindrance, and on the iPhone it can be a little more difficult to achieve that.  And if you use cinematic mode, it gets even better. Apple has been working hard on this recording and photography mode, but it feels artificial.  It's a cutout with some post-production, some post-processing done on a cell phone.  So it's not the same thing as the optical blur that we have directly on the ZV10.  So, based on that criterion, I'm going to give two points to the iPhone and three points to the ZV10.  And the next criterion is stabilization in the hand.  And I think that point really makes a big difference between the two devices, because the Sony and V10 don't come with image stabilization.  It's a shame that even in the Mark 2 version, which was a more recent release, Sony still has n't resolved this stabilization issue.  The iPhone already has very good stabilization directly during recording.  So you get much more stabilized, much better- looking takes that require less work, and sometimes even with the EV10s in hand, you'll need to put that video into editing software to get stabilization, which won't always be as good as it could be.  Therefore, based on this criterion, for this reason, the iPhone gets three points and the Zve10 gets two points.  And recently I even traveled and took the EV10s and also a gimbal to make some videos.  In the scenes I filmed handheld, it was practically impossible to keep it steady.  No matter how hard I tried to hold the camera steady and record, I could still notice this shaking in the videos, as I've already shown you here.  But in the videos I made using the EV10s on the gimbal, I had much more stabilization, resulting in a much more pleasing image to watch. So, it's a camera that ends up needing extra equipment to give you better quality.  If you record it only handheld, you'll get the feeling that you invested a lot of money in equipment, that you see people making quality images, but when you try to do it, it's not quite the same .  That's because these support devices are very helpful, and in the case of just the EV10s, recording handheld without any kind of stabilization support will be more difficult. Another criterion to consider is the audio quality of the recordings.  Both the iPhone and the EV10 have internal camera capture, but the quality is generally not the best.  To record actions or the environment around you. Of course, this audio is more than enough; it will serve you very well. Now, if you need to speak directly to the camera, like I do here, the camera's internal microphone is practically not going to cut it.  You need an external microphone that will point towards your mouth so you can speak and have that sound captured clearly.  In this regard, if you try using the internal microphone of both, I would say that the iPhone scores two, while the ZV10 scores three, because it's a camera dedicated to this and can therefore deliver better quality.  The iPhone offers some very interesting audio features, but I would still say it falls short in some aspects. So, if you need to use either of these two devices, but also need better audio quality, I've already made a video where I talk about the Holy Land Lark M2, and it works very well with both the camera with the EV10 [music] and with iPhones or other Android phones that are USB-C.  So I'll leave this video for you to watch right after this one, if you want.  Let's continue with the video.  And this next criterion, I think many people forget to consider, is the editing workflow.  When you record videos with an iPhone, there's a chance you'll also want to edit all that material directly on the iPhone and publish it on social media, or it may simply be a record of something you're going to keep.  If you're using a Sony EV10, there's a greater chance you'll want to use this material for later editing.  In that case, you'll have a slightly more complex workflow than you would with an iPhone.  That's precisely because the iPhone isn't just a camera, but a complete computer.  You can edit, manage your social media, and publish all your content directly to it.  Depending on how you're going to use these photos and videos, doing everything directly on the iPhone is a lifesaver, which is why it gets three points, while the EV10 gets two points, because the workflow will certainly be a bit more complicated and you'll always depend on downloading these photos either to your phone or computer to do the editing.  And the second-to-last criterion is also super important to take into consideration, which is the complexity of operation, that is, how easy it is to use this equipment.  In this case, the iPhone 17 Pro Max will have a significant advantage because it's the type of device that most people are already familiar with using, especially the camera.  The app will be super intuitive and the automatic mode will already deliver great quality.  However, you have other software that you can use for more advanced and professional capture, such as Final Cut or Black Magic Cameron, for example .  [music] As for the Z EV10, if you've never used a camera before and you'll have to learn the main controls, you can still easily record and photograph in automatic mode, but you 'll still need to familiarize yourself with all the menus and navigation in general to be able to explore and extract the best possible performance from the camera .  So, it's not something you can just point to and expect the best result.  You might actually need to get to know the settings a bit better .  So, once again , the iPhone gets three points, which is very much for ease of use, while the ZV10 gets two points.  And the last point, which is certainly no less important, is about the cost of getting started properly, because the value of either option will be higher.  The iPhone is expensive, and the ZV10, which is an entry-level camera in Brazil, is also expensive.  And if you consider buying lenses and other accessories, it gets even more expensive.  So you really need to assess whether you're going to use only the kit lens, what you can do compared to what you already have on your iPhone, or if you're going to invest in extra lenses.  Depending on the quality of those lenses, then DVD might be a more interesting option.  But considering the value of both, I would say the iPhone gets a score of two in this case, while the ZV10 gets a score of three, because it has a lower value with the entry-level items.  Now, if you already have an iPhone and are thinking about buying the EV10s, it might not be worth the investment, unless you've already reached the limit of what the iPhone can do and you believe the EV10s can deliver more because of the lenses, or the sensor size.  So, it's worth considering whether she'll answer you.  Or if you're still thinking about buying an iPhone, but you're also thinking about buying an EV like this.  In other words, the sum of the two purchases may not make sense to you.  So, it's worth it depending on how well each one suits your needs.  In my case, I still use the Sony EV10 and the iPhone, and it works for me to use both, mainly because, for example, if I have to shoot with the iPhone, I use the EV10 to record videos from the iPhone while I'm using the iPhone.  If I need to make videos that I record with the ZVE10, I will use it.  So, it makes sense to me that I have both devices.  And in some other scenarios, it's also very useful for me to have both.  For portability, for example, there are times when if I want to make a very quick recording, my choice will definitely be the iPhone.  Whereas if I want to do a more polished take, if I want to prioritize this background blur, as you see here, so that I can achieve this result.  So, when you have the option to choose both things, you have the best of both worlds and thus you have more flexibility. Now, if you can't choose between the two and need to decide between one or the other , my recommendation is definitely the iPhone, because it's much more flexible and much more practical to use. You can then work with the complete workflow, because it eliminates the need to have a computer as well. You can edit directly in apps on the iPhone, you can publish, and I think that's what matters. Nowadays we live in a time when social media is flooded with artificial intelligence content and a lot of useless stuff.  So, if you can prioritize quality content, content that is more human, that connects with people more than quality itself, this video was all about quality, about technical resources and what the best equipment is, but in the end what really matters is whether you have content that is relevant, that meets the people you want to connect with and that you want to communicate something to.  So, it's definitely worth thinking about.  Consider which of the two devices is better for your use case.  I hope that everything I've said here has helped you to define a better path.  And if you have any questions, leave them in the comments and I'll be sure to answer them. I'm keen to help us continue this conversation, because there's a lot I'd like to say that hasn't been said.  This video would be too long, and it's really complicated to think of all the details to bring it here for you. But I hope that these were indeed the most relevant, the most important ones.  If you enjoyed this content, consider subscribing again, and we'll see you in the next video.  Thanks, [music]

title: How to Turn off Orange Dot on iPhone 17 / 17 Pro
transcript: Hi everyone. In this short video, I will talk about the orange dot on your iPhone 17 and 17 Pro and why you can't turn it off. The orange dot shows up here whenever an app is using your microphone. This is a privacy feature in iOS, letting you know when [music] apps are accessing sensitive data. Currently, Apple does not provide a way to disable this indicator. So it will always shows up when the microphone is active. [music] I scroll down to open the control center and at the top you can see that the voice memos app is currently using the microphone. If I close the app it will disappear. Now go to settings then privacy and security and Here you can see which apps have access to your microphone. If you turn off access [music] for an app, the orange dot will no longer appear for the app. But keep in mind, you won't be able to use the microphone with the app anymore. Next, in the settings, select accessibility, then tap vocal shortcuts. [music] If there is one enabled, turn it off. As last resort, try restarting your phone. That's it. We are done. At the end of my video, let me ask you a small favor. As you can see, most of my viewers are not subscribed. [music] If you find my video helpful, please take a second to give a like and click on the subscribe button to help my videos reach more people. Thank you and see you in the next one.

title: How to Turn ON or OFF iPhone 17 / 17 Pro Max - Power ON or OFF
transcript: Hey, what's up guys? So, in this video, we will see how to turn on and off the iPhone 17, the 17 Pro or the 17 Pro Max.
